text: In this video we will demonstrate the versatility of the da Vinci operating system as we deal with an incidentally found ileal mass during a routine sigmoid resection for diverticular disease.
The surgeon performs a complete mesenteric dissection to collect lymph nodes associated with the ileal mass, using the da Vinci operating system.
The surgeon uses a ligature to divide the ileum, opting for this method over intestinal staplers for a quicker division.
The surgeon gently opens the sealed ends of the ileum and aligns them side-by-side to prepare for a side-to-side anastomosis.
The surgeon introduces a 60mm intestinal stapler into the open ends of the ileum to perform a side-to-side anastomosis, aligning the two ends for stapling.

The surgeon fires the intestinal stapler to complete the side-to-side anastomosis of the ileum. The anastomotic line is then inspected for hemostasis, ensuring there is no bleeding along the stapled tissue.
The surgeon closes the open end of the anastomosis using two firings of the intestinal stapler, ensuring the continuity of the bowel.
The surgeon closes a small mesenteric defect to prevent future bowel herniation, using sutures to secure the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon grasps the edges of the mesentery and closes the mesenteric defect using an endo loop, securing the tissue to prevent future bowel herniation.
Following resection of the ileum, the patient went on to have an uneventful robotic sigmoid resection.
